questions.stem: A 55-year-old male presents with a sudden, severe 'thunderclap' headache that began while straining on the toilet. He vomited and is now photophobic. On examination, he has neck stiffness and no focal neurological deficits. CT head shows hyperdensity in the basal cisterns. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Thunderclap headache with neck stiffness and CT hyperdensity in basal cisterns is pathognomonic for subarachnoid haemorrhage. Benign thunderclap headache would not show CT abnormalities. Meningitis typically has fever and may not be thunderclap.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old female presents with a 2-week history of a new, persistent headache. She reports scalp tenderness when brushing her hair and jaw claudication while chewing. ESR is 80 mm/h. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of new headache in an older patient with scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and elevated ESR is classic for giant cell arteritis. No other option explains jaw claudication or such a raised ESR.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old male presents with a severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. On examination, he has a petechial rash on his trunk. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic triad of fever, neck stiffness, and headache with petechial rash is highly suggestive of meningococcal meningitis (a form of bacterial meningitis). Viral meningitis is less severe and rarely causes a petechial rash.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman who is 2 weeks postpartum presents with a progressive headache over 1 week, blurred vision, and papilloedema on fundoscopy. MRI venogram shows a filling defect in the superior sagittal sinus. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Postpartum status with headache, papilloedema, and MRI evidence of sinus thrombosis is diagnostic for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension would show no thrombus on imaging.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old female presents with acute onset right-sided headache and right eyelid drooping. She has no prior headache history. Examination reveals right ptosis and miosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of acute ipsilateral headache and partial Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis) is classic for carotid artery dissection. Cluster headache also causes autonomic features but is episodic and more severe, with conjunctival injection. Pancoast tumour causes Horner's but usually not acute headache.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a sudden onset 'worst headache of life' 6 hours ago. Neurological examination is normal. A non-contrast CT head is performed and reported as normal by the radiologist. What is the single most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In suspected subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) with a negative CT head within 12 hours of onset, lumbar puncture is the gold standard to detect xanthochromia, confirming the diagnosis. CT angiogram may identify an aneurysm but is not diagnostic of SAH itself.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known HIV (CD4 count 120 cells/μL) presents with headache, fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. He is alert and oriented (GCS 15) with no focal neurological signs or papilloedema. What is the single most appropriate investigation before performing a lumbar puncture?
questions.explanation: In immunocompromised patients (e.g. HIV) with suspected meningitis, NICE guidelines recommend CT head without contrast prior to lumbar puncture to exclude space-occupying lesions or raised intracranial pressure, even in the absence of focal signs.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of progressive headache, worse in the morning and on Valsalva manoeuvre. On examination, she has bilateral papilloedema but no focal neurological deficit. What is the single most appropriate first investigation?
questions.explanation: In suspected raised intracranial pressure with papilloedema, a non-contrast CT head is the first-line investigation to rule out a mass lesion or hydrocephalus before considering lumbar puncture. MRI is more sensitive but not the initial test in an emergency setting.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of bitemporal headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication. ESR is 85 mm/hr and CRP is 45 mg/L. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard for confirming giant cell arteritis, showing granulomatous inflammation. Although treatment is often started based on clinical and laboratory findings, biopsy is definitive when positive.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a sudden onset left-sided headache and an ipsilateral Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis) after a vigorous tennis match. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: MR angiography of the head and neck
questions.explanation: In suspected carotid artery dissection presenting with headache and Horner's syndrome, MR angiography (or CT angiography) of the head and neck is the investigation of choice to visualise the dissection flap or mural haematoma. Carotid Doppler is less sensitive for intracranial dissection.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of new-onset headache, scalp tenderness, and jaw claudication. ESR is 85 mm/h. Temporal artery biopsy shows giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Giant cell arteritis is an emergency due to risk of blindness; high-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone) are first-line. NSAIDs, low-dose aspirin, or steroid-sparing agents are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a BMI of 33 presents with persistent headache and blurred vision. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema. MRI brain is normal. Lumbar puncture opening pressure is 32 cmH2O. Diagnosis: idiopathic intracranial hypertension. What is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy?
questions.explanation: Acetazolamide reduces CSF production and is first-line medical therapy for IIH. Topiramate is an alternative but not first-line; diuretics and corticosteroids are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known chronic migraine presents with severe headache, fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. Blood cultures grow Streptococcus pneumoniae. CSF analysis confirms bacterial meningitis. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?
questions.explanation: For community-acquired bacterial meningitis, ceftriaxone is first-line (covers pneumococcus, meningococcus, Haemophilus). Amoxicillin is added if Listeria suspected; metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, and vancomycin are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a thunderclap headache. CT head shows subarachnoid haemorrhage from a ruptured posterior communicating artery aneurysm. She undergoes endovascular coiling on day 1. On day 3, she is stable. Which drug should be given to prevent delayed cerebral ischaemia?
questions.explanation: Nimodipine is the only agent shown to reduce poor outcomes from vasospasm after SAH. Aspirin, heparin, mannitol, and phenytoin are not standard prophylaxis for delayed cerebral ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old immunocompetent woman presents with a 3-day history of headache, fever, and confusion. MRI brain shows temporal lobe hyperintensities. CSF PCR is positive for herpes simplex virus type 1. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Oral valaciclovir 1 g three times daily
questions.explanation: Herpes simplex encephalitis is treated with high-dose IV aciclovir. Oral valaciclovir is inadequate; ganciclovir is for CMV; ceftriaxone is for bacterial; steroids are adjunctive but not primary.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a sudden onset severe headache ('thunderclap') that occurred 2 hours ago. He has neck stiffness and photophobia. GCS 15, no focal neurological signs. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Thunderclap headache with neck stiffness and photophobia raises suspicion of subarachnoid haemorrhage. Immediate CT head without contrast is first-line to detect SAH; it is most sensitive within first 6 hours. Lumbar puncture is indicated if CT negative but not immediate first step. Antibiotics and steroids are for meningitis and GCA respectively.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with known idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), on acetazolamide, presents with worsening headache and new-onset blurred vision. Fundoscopy reveals papilloedema. She has no focal neurology. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for optic nerve sheath fenestration
Perform urgent lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement
questions.explanation: In IIH with worsening vision, urgent lumbar puncture with CSF drainage is both diagnostic (opening pressure) and therapeutic (reducing intracranial pressure). Increasing acetazolamide is not immediate if vision declining. CT venogram may be needed if not already done, but not first. Steroids are not indicated. Surgery is later.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a 12-hour history of headache, fever (39°C), and neck stiffness. GCS 14, no rash, no focal signs. Blood cultures are drawn. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected bacterial meningitis without focal signs or contraindications, immediate empirical IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and dexamethasone should be given after blood cultures, without waiting for CT or LP. CT is indicated only if focal signs or GCS <12. Acyclovir for viral, prednisolone for GCA, LP can be delayed.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of right-sided headache, scalp tenderness, and pain on chewing (jaw claudication). She has no visual symptoms. ESR 80 mm/hr. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: Giant cell arteritis is suspected based on age, headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and raised ESR. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone) should be started to prevent ischaemic complications, particularly visual loss. Biopsy should be arranged urgently but not delay steroids. Imaging and other treatments are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a 5-year history of migraine with typical visual aura presents with a severe headache similar to her usual migraines, but the aura lasted 2 hours (her typical aura duration is 30 minutes). She has no focal neurological signs and is systemically well. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Prescribe a triptan for acute attack
questions.explanation: A prolonged or atypical aura in a migraineur warrants urgent neuroimaging to exclude secondary causes such as arteriovenous malformation or ischaemia. While treatment of acute migraine is important, the atypical feature mandates investigation first. Steroids, LP, and prophylaxis are not appropriate at this stage.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a sudden, severe headache that reached maximal intensity within seconds. She describes it as the worst headache of her life. There is no neck stiffness or photophobia. Which single feature in this history is most suggestive of a life-threatening cause?
options: Headache reaching maximal intensity within seconds
questions.explanation: A thunderclap headache that peaks within seconds is the hallmark of subarachnoid haemorrhage. Other options are non-specific or common in benign headaches. The absence of neck stiffness does not rule out SAH.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student presents with a 24-hour history of headache, fever, and generalised myalgia. On examination, temperature is 39.2°C and there is nuchal rigidity. There is no rash or focal neurological deficit. Which single clinical sign in this patient is the red flag for a bacterial infection requiring urgent investigation?
questions.explanation: Nuchal rigidity (neck stiffness) is a classic sign of meningeal irritation and, along with fever and headache, strongly suggests bacterial meningitis. While photophobia is also a red flag, the presence of nuchal rigidity on examination is more specific and indicates urgent investigation.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with a new-onset headache over the past two weeks, associated with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication when eating. He also reports some subjective fever. Which single laboratory finding is most indicative of the underlying diagnosis and its need for urgent treatment?
questions.explanation: A markedly elevated ESR (often >50 mm/h) is a hallmark of giant cell arteritis, especially in the setting of typical symptoms like scalp tenderness and jaw claudication. CRP is also elevated but less specific than ESR in GCA. Urgent corticosteroids are needed to prevent blindness.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known HIV infection (not on antiretroviral therapy, CD4 count 100 cells/μL) presents with a 1-week history of gradual-onset headache, and now develops new-onset weakness in his right arm and leg. Which single clinical finding on examination is the most important red flag for a life-threatening intracranial process?
options: Focal motor deficit of the right side
questions.explanation: In an immunocompromised patient with headache, a new focal neurological deficit (like hemiparesis) is a red flag for a space-occupying lesion such as cerebral toxoplasmosis or primary CNS lymphoma. While papilloedema indicates raised ICP, a focal deficit is more localising and prompts urgent imaging.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with no prior headache history presents to the GP with a 2-week history of daily headaches. She has also noticed some visual disturbance with blurring of vision. On examination, fundoscopy reveals disc swelling in both eyes. Which single ocular finding is the red flag requiring immediate further investigation?
questions.explanation: Bilateral papilloedema (disc swelling) indicates raised intracranial pressure, which is a red flag necessitating urgent brain imaging and further investigation. The other findings may be seen in hypertension or diabetic retinopathy but are not the primary emergency in this context.
